text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real react-hook-form repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: when a form is created in the disabled state (`useForm({ disabled: true })`), calling `setValue(name, value, { shouldDirty: true })` fails to mark anything dirty. After registering a field and then programmatically setting a value that differs from its default with `shouldDirty: true`, `getFieldState(name).isDirty`, `formState.isDirty`, and `formState.dirtyFields` all incorrectly stay in their pristine state — even though the new value clearly differs from the default. Conversely, programmatically setting the field back to its default value on a disabled form should clear that dirty state again. The root cause is that the dirty-tracking path treats a disabled form as 'never track dirtiness' and swallows the update entirely. The correct behavior is that an explicit programmatic dirty request (`setValue` with `shouldDirty: true`) is honored even while the form is disabled, while ordinary user interaction (typing/blur) on a disabled form still does NOT track dirtiness. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (via `npm install -g pnpm@10.34.5 && CYPRESS_INSTALL_BINARY=0 pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts`). Run tests with `node_modules/.bin/jest --config ./scripts/jest/jest.config.js src/__tests__/useForm/formState.test.tsx`.

message: `--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.
`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: I’ll trace the disabled-form dirty-state path from `setValue` through the form state update helpers, then patch the smallest library-level condition and run the targeted Jest suite.

text: The relevant gate is in `src/logic/createFormControl.ts`: `updateTouchAndDirty` currently exits all dirty work when `_options.disabled` is true, while `setValue` already passes `shouldDirty` into that helper. I’m checking the surrounding event and subscription logic to preserve disabled user-input behavior while allowing only explicit programmatic dirty updates.

6112441b 🐞 fix #13569 calling reset triggers subscribe with latest name instead of undefined (#13574)

text: The existing disabled test covers user-facing disabled behavior and does not exercise `setValue(..., { shouldDirty: true })`. The narrow fix is to let `updateTouchAndDirty` run when `shouldDirty` is explicitly true, while keeping the current outer guard for change/blur events and for `shouldTouch` alone. I’ll add focused regression coverage for setting dirty and clearing it back to the default, including `getFieldState`.

message: Reconnecting... 1/5 (stream disconnected before completion: Concurrency limit exceeded for user, please retry later)

text: I’m applying the guard change in `createFormControl.ts` and adding a regression test in the existing form-state suite. The test will subscribe to both aggregate dirty properties, register the field, assert all three dirty views after a programmatic change, then assert they clear when restored to the default.

text: The source and regression test are patched. The targeted Jest process is still running; once it completes I’ll inspect the diff and, if needed, run the narrower related tests to catch any interaction with disabled field arrays or user events.

The first test run exposed a second disabled gate: `_getDirty()` itself returns `false` whenever the form is disabled. That leaves field-level `dirtyFields` correct but prevents aggregate `formState.isDirty` from becoming true. I’m removing that gate as well; user events remain blocked by `updateTouchAndDirty`, so this does not re-enable dirty tracking for typing or blur.
